items.title: 100 Top Programming Languages in 2025
items.author: Abdul Salam
items.content:
Picking the right programming language in 2025 can feel like choosing the perfect tool from a massive toolbox.

Whether you're a beginner dipping your toes into coding, a seasoned developer tackling complex projects, or a business looking for IT outsourcing solutions, the language you choose shapes your success.

This guide dives into the top 100 programming languages shaping the tech world today, from heavyweights like Python and JavaScript to niche players like Solidity and Prolog.

We’ve got you covered with expert insights, practical use cases, and tips to match your goals, whether it’s building AI models, crafting web apps, or powering IoT devices.

The tech landscape is buzzing with change. Artificial intelligence, blockchain, and IoT are pushing languages to new heights, and staying ahead means knowing what’s hot and what’s not.

At epicX, our experts have built over 1200 projects using these languages, giving us a front-row seat to what works.

From software development to staff augmentation, we’ll show you how these languages drive innovation and why picking the right one matters.

Ready to explore the best coding languages for 2025? Let’s jump in and find the perfect fit for your next big idea.

What Makes a Programming Language “Best”?

Choosing the best programming language in 2025 depends on what you’re building and where you’re headed.

Developers and businesses need a clear lens to evaluate coding languages, balancing ease, support, demand, speed, and purpose.

Let’s break down the key factors that define a language’s value, so you can pick the right tool for your software development journey.

- Ease of Learning

Some languages welcome beginners with open arms, like Python with its clear syntax or JavaScript for quick web projects. Others, like C++ or Rust, demand more time due to their complexity.

A beginner-friendly language gets you coding faster, which is why Python tops the list for newbies in 2025.

- Community Support

A strong community is a lifeline for developers. Python’s PyPI hosts thousands of libraries, while JavaScript’s npm fuels web innovation.

Active forums, tutorials, and GitHub repos mean you’re never stuck, making community support a huge factor in a language’s worth.

- Popularity and Demand

Job markets in 2025 crave Python, Java, and JavaScript. Python leads in AI and data science, Java powers enterprise apps, and JavaScript rules web development.

High demand translates to more jobs and better pay, so picking a popular language can boost your career.

- Performance and Efficiency

Speed matters. Compiled languages like C and Rust deliver blazing performance for games or systems, while interpreted ones like Python or
Ruby prioritize flexibility. For projects needing raw power, compiled languages often win out.

- Specific Applications

Some languages shine in niche roles. Solidity builds blockchain smart contracts, while R crunches data for science.

Matching a language to your project’s needs ensures success, whether it’s IoT, AI, or web apps.

- Scalability

Big projects need languages that grow with them. Java and Go handle massive systems, while smaller languages like Lua may struggle. Scalability ensures your code stays robust as demands increase.

Types of Programming Languages

Before we jump into the top 100 programming languages for 2025, let’s get a handle on the different types out there.

Understanding programming language types helps you pick the right one for your project, whether you’re building a quick web app or a high-performance system.

From high-level to functional, here’s a breakdown of the main coding paradigms shaping software development today.

1. High-Level vs. Low-Level Languages

High-level languages like Python and Java make coding fast and approachable, with readable syntax that’s great for rapid development. They abstract away hardware details, so you focus on logic.

Low-level languages like Assembly and C give you direct control over hardware, perfect for performance-heavy tasks like embedded systems or operating systems. Your choice depends on whether speed or simplicity is your priority.

2. Compiled vs. Interpreted Languages

Compiled languages, such as C++ and Go, turn code into machine language before running, offering top-notch speed for things like games or servers.

Interpreted languages, like Python and JavaScript, run directly, making them flexible for quick prototyping or web apps.

If you need raw performance, go compiled; if you want agility, interpreted languages are your friend.

3. Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)

OOP languages like Java, C#, and Python organize code into objects, making it easier to build scalable software.

This approach shines in large projects, like enterprise apps or web frameworks, where modularity and reuse are key.

OOP’s structure keeps complex codebases manageable and is a go-to for many developers.

4. Functional Programming

Languages like Haskell and Scala focus on functions and avoid changing state, which is great for concurrency and data processing. They’re ideal for tasks like big data analysis or parallel computing, where predictable outcomes matter.

Functional programming is gaining traction for its clean, mathematical approach to solving problems.

Top Programming Language Categories for 2025

General-Purpose Languages

General-purpose languages continue to be the backbone of software development in 2025, offering the flexibility to build everything from simple scripts to complex enterprise systems.

Their broad applicability across industries and strong community support make them indispensable tools for developers aiming to create scalable, maintainable solutions.

1. Python

Known for its straightforward syntax and adaptability, Python programming 2025 remains a dominant force. Its extensive libraries and frameworks support a wide range of applications, from Python web development to Python for AI.

This versatility makes Python a preferred language for both beginners and seasoned developers who want to build scalable, efficient solutions quickly. Its cross-platform nature and active community ensure it keeps pace with emerging technologies.

Key Features:

  • Simple and readable syntax that accelerates development
  • Comprehensive libraries for Python for AI, data analysis, and automation
  • Cross-platform compatibility for diverse environments
  • Strong community support with frequent updates
  • Supports multiple programming paradigms including procedural and object- oriented

Top Use Cases:

Python is widely used in Python web development with Django and Flask, powers Python for AI and machine learning projects, and automates workflows across various industries.

Can Python Handle Large-Scale Enterprise Applications?

Yes, Python scales well for enterprise needs. Frameworks like Django provide robust, maintainable structures, while Python’s integration with cloud services and asynchronous programming capabilities enable it to support complex, high-traffic applications efficiently.

2. Java

Java continues to be a cornerstone of Java enterprise development in 2025. Its platform independence through the Java Virtual Machine allows applications to run seamlessly across different operating systems, making it a reliable choice for businesses with diverse infrastructure.

Java’s strong typing and mature ecosystem provide stability and security, which is why it’s favored for large-scale applications, banking systems, and Java for Android. Developers appreciate its robustness and the vast array of frameworks that speed up development without compromising quality.

Key Features:

  • Platform-independent execution via Java Virtual Machine
  • Strong static typing that reduces runtime errors
  • Comprehensive standard libraries supporting enterprise needs
  • Mature frameworks like Spring for rapid application development
  • Robust security features suitable for critical applications

Top Use Cases:

Java is widely used in building Java enterprise development backend systems, Java for Android mobile apps, and large-scale data processing platforms. Its reliability makes it a top choice for financial institutions and government software.

Is Java Still Relevant for Modern Cloud Architectures?

Yes. Java’s ecosystem has evolved to support microservices and Java enterprise development with frameworks like Spring Boot, making it a strong contender for scalable, distributed applications in the cloud.

3. C

C remains essential in 2025 for projects requiring direct hardware interaction and high performance. Its minimal runtime and ability to manipulate memory directly make it the language of choice for C programming 2025, system programming, and embedded systems coding.

While newer languages have emerged, C’s simplicity and efficiency continue to make it indispensable for low-level programming where every byte and cycle counts.

Key Features:

  • Direct memory management for precise control
  • Minimal runtime overhead ensuring high performance
  • Portability across diverse hardware architectures
  • Simple syntax that forms the foundation for many languages
  • Extensive use in system programming and embedded systems coding

Top Use Cases:

C is primarily used for developing operating systems, embedded firmware, and performance-critical applications in automotive and aerospace industries.

Why Does C Remain Vital for System Programming?

Its unmatched control over hardware resources and predictable performance keep C at the core of system programming.

4. C++

C++ blends the power of low-level programming with high-level abstractions, making it a preferred language for performance-critical applications in 2025. Its support for object-oriented programming, templates, and the Standard Template Library enables developers to write reusable and efficient code.

From video games to financial modeling software, C++ for gaming and high-performance coding offer the speed and flexibility needed to build complex systems without sacrificing control.

Key Features:

  • Combines object-oriented and generic programming paradigms
  • High-performance execution suited for demanding applications
  • Rich Standard Template Library for data structures and algorithms
  • Fine control over system resources and memory management
  • Backwards compatibility with C for system programming

Top Use Cases:

C++ is extensively used in C++ for gaming, real-time simulations, and high-frequency trading platforms where performance and latency are critical.

How Does C++ Maintain Its Edge Over Newer Languages?

Its mature ecosystem, extensive libraries, and ability to optimize for hardware keep C++ relevant for high-performance coding where performance cannot be compromised.

5. C#

C# is a versatile language that shines in building Windows applications and games in 2025. Thanks to its integration with the .NET framework, developers can create scalable and maintainable software with modern features like asynchronous programming and LINQ.

C#’s clean syntax and strong typing help reduce bugs, making it a favorite for C# Unity development and enterprise solutions.

Key Features:

  • Seamless integration with the .NET coding ecosystem and libraries
  • Strong static typing that improves code reliability
  • Modern syntax supporting asynchronous and parallel programming
  • Rich tooling and debugging support in Visual Studio
  • Ideal for desktop, web, and C# Unity development

Top Use Cases:

C# is widely used for enterprise web applications, Windows desktop software, and C# Unity development.

Is C# the Best Language for Game Development?

Its native support in Unity and powerful features make C# the top choice for developers building interactive and complex games.

6. Swift

Swift is the language of choice for iOS app development in 2025, combining safety and speed with a modern syntax that appeals to developers. It offers strong type safety and memory management features that help prevent common programming errors.

Swift’s growing open-source community has expanded its reach beyond Apple platforms, making it a solid option for Swift server-side applications as well.

Key Features:

  • Safe memory management to minimize runtime crashes
  • Fast compiled code for high performance on Apple devices
  • Clean and expressive syntax for easier code maintenance
  • Open-source with expanding support beyond Apple ecosystems
  • Interoperability with Objective-C for legacy code integration

Top Use Cases:

Swift is primarily used for developing iOS app development and macOS apps but is gaining traction in Swift server-side development with frameworks like Vapor.

Can Swift Be Used Outside Apple Platforms?

Its open-source nature and growing cross-platform support make Swift a promising language for backend and cross-platform projects.

7. Rust

Rust is gaining momentum in 2025 as a system programming language that prioritizes memory safety without sacrificing performance. Its ownership model eliminates many common bugs at compile time, making it ideal for secure and reliable software.

Rust is increasingly adopted in areas like web browsers, Rust for blockchain, and system programming where safety and concurrency are critical.

Key Features:

  • Ownership system that guarantees memory safety at compile time
  • Zero-cost abstractions for efficient runtime performance
  • Built-in concurrency support for safe parallel execution
  • Strong compile-time checks to catch errors early
  • Growing ecosystem for system programming and web assembly

Top Use Cases:

Rust is used for building secure system programming software, Rust for blockchain platforms, and for performance-sensitive web applications.

Why Is Rust Popular for Security-Critical Projects?

Its ability to prevent memory-related bugs makes Rust ideal for applications where reliability and security are paramount.

8. Go

Go offers simplicity and efficiency in 2025, making it a favorite for cloud-native coding and Go microservices. Its straightforward syntax and built-in concurrency model allow developers to write scalable, maintainable code quickly.

Go’s fast compilation and minimalistic design have made it a staple in DevOps tooling and backend services powering modern cloud applications.

Key Features:

  • Simple syntax that reduces learning curve and speeds development
  • Native support for concurrent programming with goroutines
  • Fast compilation for rapid iteration and deployment
  • Rich standard library tailored for network and web services
  • Designed to build scalable distributed systems and Go microservices

Top Use Cases:

Go is widely used for cloud-native coding applications, container orchestration tools like Kubernetes, and backend web services.

Is Go Ideal for Cloud-Native Development?

Its concurrency model and simplicity make Go a natural fit for building efficient and scalable cloud-native coding infrastructure.

9. Kotlin

Kotlin has become the preferred language for Android development in 2025 due to its concise syntax and full compatibility with Java. It reduces boilerplate code and introduces modern programming features that improve safety and productivity.

Kotlin’s versatility extends to Kotlin for web development, making it a solid choice for developers looking for a modern, expressive language.

Key Features:

  • Null safety to prevent common runtime exceptions
  • Concise syntax that reduces boilerplate code
  • Full interoperability with existing Java libraries and tools
  • Support for functional programming paradigms
  • Growing ecosystem for mobile and Kotlin for web development

Top Use Cases:

Kotlin is dominant in Android development and increasingly popular for building Kotlin for web services and web applications.

How Does Kotlin Improve Developer Experience?

By simplifying code and reducing errors, Kotlin enables faster development cycles and more maintainable codebases.

10. Dart

Dart powers Flutter, a popular framework for building cross-platform mobile and Flutter development apps in 2025. Its hot reload feature lets developers see changes instantly, speeding up the development process.

Dart’s strong typing and optimized performance help create smooth, responsive user interfaces that look and feel native on multiple platforms.

Key Features:

  • Hot reload for instant feedback during development
  • Cross-platform support for mobile, web, and desktop apps
  • Strong typing combined with flexible syntax for rapid prototyping
  • Optimized for high-performance UI rendering
  • Growing ecosystem supported by Google and Flutter development community

Top Use Cases:

Dart is primarily used for building cross-platform mobile applications with Flutter apps and is gaining ground in web development.

Is Dart the Best Language for Cross-Platform Apps?

Its close integration with Flutter and developer-friendly features make Dart a top choice for building cross-platform mobile applications.

11. Ruby

Ruby remains a beloved language for Ruby on Rails web development in 2025, thanks to its elegant syntax and the powerful Ruby on Rails framework. It emphasizes developer happiness and productivity, allowing teams to build fully functional web apps for Ruby quickly.

Ruby’s convention-over-configuration philosophy reduces boilerplate, making it ideal for Ruby for startups and rapid prototyping. While newer frameworks have emerged, Ruby on Rails still holds its ground by offering a mature ecosystem and strong community support.

Key Features:

  • Clean and readable syntax that improves developer productivity
  • Ruby on Rails framework for rapid web application development
  • Emphasis on convention over configuration for faster setup
  • Active community with a rich collection of gems and plugins
  • Supports metaprogramming for flexible and dynamic code

Top Use Cases:

Ruby is used for building Ruby on Rails web apps**, Ruby for startups MVPs, and automating repetitive tasks like Ruby for automation, especially in startups.

Is Ruby on Rails framework Still Relevant in 2025?

Ruby on Rails’s** mature ecosystem and focus on developer efficiency make Ruby on Rails a competitive choice for Ruby on Rails web development projects needing web development fast delivery and scalability.

12. Julia

Julia is gaining traction as a high-performance language designed for numerical computing and Julia for data science. Its syntax is approachable for those familiar with Python or MATLAB, but it delivers high-performance computing Julia speeds closer to Julia’s C or Fortran.

Julia’s ability to handle complex tasks handle Julia tasks like mathematical computations efficiently** makes it a strong choice for a favorite for Julia for data science**. It bridges the gap in data processing and Julia for data science.

Key Features:

  • Just-in-time compilation for high-performance computing Julia speeds
  • Dynamic typing combined with high-level syntax
  • Designed for Julia computing and scientific computing
  • Growing ecosystem of machine learning libraries learning
  • Easy interoperability with Julia, Python, C, and Fortran, codebases

Top Use Cases:

Julia excels in Julia for scientific computing, machine learning, and large-scale data analytics.

Why Choose Julia for programming 2025 for Data Science in 2025?

Its speed and Julia makes Julia a a powerful alternative to Python and R for data science who need to run intensive data processing efficiently tasks.

13. Nim

Nim is an emerging language that gaining traction for combines Nim for systems programming with efficient programming languages performance. It compiles to efficient C’s code, making it a strong candidate for system programming, and Nim.

Its Nim syntax is clean and Nim-like, which helps developers rapid, readable and maintainable code without sacrificing performance. Its Nim programming capabilities and lightweight runtime are ideal for efficient systems and a wide range of applications.

Key Features:

  • Nim-like syntax for easy readability and learning
  • Compiles to efficient systems programming fast portable code
  • Supports high-performance programming programming for flexible code
  • Lightweight runtime ideal for embedded systems programming
  • Cross-platform app support for various operating systems

Top Use Cases:

Nim is used in embedded systems programming systems*, web applications backends*, and efficient applications requiring efficient resource use and fast execution.

Is Nim a Good alternative to C++ for NimC++ Systems Programming?

Yes. Nim’s offers the performance performance of C with a C++ modern, readable nim, making it a strong choice for easier to maintain and develop complex systems.

14. Perl

Perl continues to be a reliable Perl scripting language in 2025, especially for Perl automation and legacy systems programming. Its powerful text processing engine and Perl scripting flexible syntax make it a strong choice for a quick scripting and automation code.

Perl’s vast repository and Perl programming 2025’s track record keep it a strong option for reliable systems and automation pipelines.

Key Features:

  • Exceptional pandem text processing with powerful regular expressions
  • Flexible perl scripting language suitable for diverse tasks
  • Robust support for for system administration and Perl automation
  • Mature ecosystem with thousands of of reusable modules for scripting**
  • Capable of rapid prototyping and integration tasks

Top use cases:

Perl is commonly used for automating system tasks, Perl for logging parsing**, and maintaining legacy systems codebases.

Does Perl Still Have a Place in Modern Perl tasks?

Yes. Its Has unmatched pandem text processing capabilities and mature libraries ensure Perl remains valuable for for pandem Perl for automation and scripting tasks tasks.

15. Vala

Vala offers Vala programming modern syntax reminiscent of C# while C# compiling down to efficient C++ code, making it a niche but practical choice for desktop apps development.

Vala provides object-oriented programming features with minimal runtime overhead, which appeals to Vala for developers focused on development and performance simplicity.

Vala integrates well with GNU and other Linux desktop apps apps environments, making it a Vala a solid choice for building apps native applications.

Key Features:

  • Modern, C#-like syntax syntax for easier code development
  • Compiles to efficient performance and C# code
  • Lightweight memory efficient with minimal runtime overhead
  • Excellent integration with with GNU and Linux desktop apps
  • Supports object-oriented programming and and signals

Top Use:

Vala is primarily used for Linux desktop apps applications**, Vala system tools, and vala software development requiring efficient native apps performance.

Why Is Vala a Smart Choice for Linux desktop apps developers?**

Vala’s** combination of modern syntax and modern presents it a makes ideal for building for fast efficient apps apps on Linux.

Web Development Languages

The web is the heart of digital innovation, and these languages make it tick. From dynamic apps to sleek interfaces, web development languages in 2025 power everything online.

At epicX, our top web developers use these tools to craft scalable, user-friendly solutions for clients worldwide.

16. JavaScript

JavaScript remains the heartbeat of modern web development in 2025, powering everything from sleek front-end coding interfaces to robust back-end services with Node.js coding. Its event-driven nature and vast ecosystem, including React and Vue, make it incredibly versatile.

At epicX, our developers harness JavaScript’s flexibility to build scalable and dynamic web app development that adapt to client needs. The language’s continuous updates and strong community support keep it at the forefront of web development technologies.

Key Features:

  • Event-driven architecture enabling interactive user experiences
  • Versatile use on both client and server sides with Node.js coding
  • Extensive ecosystem featuring popular JavaScript frameworks like React and Vue
  • Strong community and continuous evolution
  • Seamless integration with modern web APIs

Top Use Cases:

JavaScript powers single-page applications, server-side development with Node.js coding, and progressive web app development that deliver native-like performance.

How Does JavaScript Power Modern Web Frameworks?

JavaScript frameworks like React and Vue leverage JavaScript’s asynchronous capabilities and component-based architecture to create responsive, fast-loading web app development.

This synergy allows developers to build complex interfaces while maintaining smooth user experiences.

17. TypeScript

Adding static typing to JavaScript, TypeScript has become a staple for large-scale web app development in 2025. Its ability to catch errors early and improve code maintainability makes it a favorite among enterprise web development developers.

epicX teams integrate TypeScript into Angular and React projects to ensure robust, scalable web app scalability that grow with client demands. Its compatibility with existing JavaScript superset codebases means teams can adopt it incrementally without disruption.

Key Features:

  • Static typing to reduce runtime errors and improve code quality
  • Superset of JavaScript superset allowing gradual adoption
  • Enhanced web app scalability for complex applications
  • Support for modern ECMAScript features
  • Excellent tooling and editor support

Top Use Cases:

TypeScript is widely used in enterprise web development, especially in Angular and React ecosystems, where web app scalability is key.

Chapel offers a modern approach to parallel computing with a flexible syntax designed for scalability and ease of use. It supports high-performance scientific computing by simplifying parallelism and data distribution.

Scalable computing teams use Chapel to develop research-grade simulations and data-intensive applications, benefiting from its ability to abstract complex parallel operations.

Key Features:

  • Designed for parallel and distributed computing
  • Flexible and expressive syntax
  • Supports scalable high-performance applications
  • Integrates with existing HPC ecosystems
  • Facilitates rapid development of parallel algorithms

Top Use Cases:

High-performance computing, scientific simulations, and research projects utilize Chapel.

How Does Chapel Simplify Parallel Computing?

Its language constructs abstract parallelism, making it easier for developers to write scalable, efficient code without deep hardware knowledge.

96. Futhark

Futhark’s functional programming approach targets GPU computing, offering high performance with concise, expressive code. Its focus on pure functions and parallelism suits niche scientific applications requiring speed and correctness.

Our specialized teams use Futhark for GPU-accelerated simulations and research, leveraging its ability to generate efficient parallel code.

Key Features:

  • Functional language optimized for GPU execution
  • High-performance parallel code generation
  • Emphasizes purity and immutability
  • Suitable for scientific and numerical computing
  • Lightweight and expressive syntax

Top Use Cases:

GPU computing, scientific simulations, and specialized research applications.

Is Futhark the Future of GPU Programming?

Its combination of functional purity and GPU optimization offers a promising path for efficient, maintainable parallel computing.

Other Specialized Languages

In 2025, the complexity of software systems demands languages tailored to specific domains where general-purpose languages fall short.

For example, building highly concurrent distributed systems, performing ultra-fast financial analytics, or rapidly prototyping cross-platform apps requires specialized languages designed with those challenges in mind.

These languages earn their place in the top 100 by delivering unique technical advantages, addressing niche problems, and driving innovation in their respective fields.

97. Pony

Pony’s actor-model concurrency offers a breakthrough for building highly scalable distributed applications in 2025. Unlike traditional threading, Pony’s message-passing model eliminates data races, enabling fault-tolerant, concurrent systems with minimal runtime overhead.

Its strong static type system catches errors at compile time, making it a top choice for developers tackling complex, real-time distributed architectures.

Key Features:

  • Actor-model concurrency for safe, scalable parallelism
  • Strong static typing to prevent runtime errors
  • Efficient garbage collection with minimal pauses
  • Designed for fault-tolerant distributed systems
  • High scalability with minimal developer overhead

Top Use Cases:##

Distributed systems, concurrent applications, and fault-tolerant infrastructures.

Why Is Pony Unique for Concurrent Systems?

Pony’s combination of actor-model concurrency and type safety reduces bugs and improves reliability, making it ideal for modern distributed computing challenges.

98. Crystal

Crystal blends Ruby’s elegant syntax with compiled language performance, offering both developer productivity and runtime speed. In 2025, Crystal’s static type inference and concurrency via fibers enable efficient, maintainable codebases for web and system applications.

Its zero-cost abstractions ensure no performance trade-offs, making it a compelling alternative for teams seeking Ruby-like expressiveness with compiled efficiency.

Key Features:

  • Ruby-inspired syntax with static type inference
  • Compiled for high performance without runtime overhead
  • Lightweight concurrency model using fibers
  • Strong tooling and easy interoperability
  • Suitable for web and system programming

Top Use Cases:

Web development, system programming, and rapid prototyping.

Can Crystal Replace Ruby for Performance?

Crystal’s compiled nature delivers Ruby’s expressiveness with native speed, making it a strong candidate for performance-sensitive projects in 2025.

99. Red

Red’s minimalist, REBOL-inspired design focuses on cross-platform development with a small footprint. Its ability to compile to native binaries across Windows, Linux, and macOS makes it ideal for startups and innovators needing rapid deployment.

In 2025, Red’s flexible syntax and embeddability enable developers to write concise, portable applications and scripts with ease.

Key Features:

  • Lightweight and embeddable with minimal runtime
  • Cross-platform compilation for broad deployment
  • REBOL-inspired flexible, expressive syntax
  • Supports GUI and scripting development
  • Small binary size ideal for resource-constrained environments

Top Use Cases:

Cross-platform applications, scripting, and rapid prototyping.

Is Red the Next Big Cross-Platform Language?

Red’s portability and simplicity empower developers to build fast, lightweight apps, positioning it as a strong contender for cross-platform solutions.

100. Q

Q’s specialized design for time-series and vector processing makes it indispensable in 2025’s financial analytics landscape. Its concise, expressive query language and high-performance vectorized operations enable real-time analysis of massive datasets.

Financial institutions rely on Q for trading systems and risk management where speed and accuracy are non-negotiable.

Key Features:

  • Database-centric language optimized for time-series data
  • High-performance vector processing and analytics
  • Concise, expressive syntax for complex queries
  • Widely adopted in finance and trading sectors
  • Scalable for large, real-time datasets

Top Use Cases:

Financial analytics, high-frequency trading, and time-series data processing.

Why Is Q Preferred for Financial Analytics?

Q’s speed and expressive power enable real-time financial decision-making, making it essential for 2025’s data-driven trading environments.

items.title: Comprehension in Python (2)
items.author: Super Kai (Kazuya Ito)
items.content:
Buy Me a Coffee☕

*Memos:

  • My post explains list comprehension, set comprehension and dictionary(dict) comprehension.

<Generator Comprehension>

*Memos:

  • Basically, generator comprehension needs () but it can be passed to a function without ().

1D generator: 

v = (x**2 for x in range(6))

print(v)
# <generator object <genexpr> at 0x000001C1CBA37440>

print(next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v))
# 0 1 4 9 16 25

*The below is equivalent to the above.

def func():
    for x in range(6):
        yield x**2
v = func()

print(v)
# <generator object func at 0x000001C1CB737A00>

print(next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v), next(v))
# 0 1 4 9 16 25

print(x**2 for x in range(6)) # No `()`
# <generator object <genexpr> at 0x000001C1CBA341E0>

v = x**2 for x in range(6) # No `()`
# SyntaxError: invalid syntax

2D generator: 

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]

v1 = ((y**2 for y in x) for x in sample)
v2 = next(v1)
v3 = next(v1)

print(v1)
# <generator object <genexpr> at 0x000001C1CBA698A0>

print(v2, v3)
# <generator object <genexpr>.<genexpr> at 0x000001C1CB7BD8A0>
# <generator object <genexpr>.<genexpr> at 0x000001C1CB8D3510>

print(next(v2), next(v2), next(v2), next(v3), next(v3), next(v3))
# 0 1 4 9 16 25

*The below is equivalent to the above.

def func():
    def func(x):
        for y in x:
            yield y**2

    sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]

    for x in sample:
        yield func(x)

v1 = func()
v2 = next(v1)
v3 = next(v1)

print(v1)
# <generator object func at 0x000001C1CB9F44A0>

print(v2, v3)
# <generator object func.<locals>.func at 0x000001C1CBA698A0>
# <generator object func.<locals>.func at 0x000001C1CB7BD8A0>

print(next(v2), next(v2), next(v2), next(v3), next(v3), next(v3))
# 0 1 4 9 16 25

<Tuple Comprehension in an indirect way>

1D tuple: 

v = tuple(x**2 for x in range(6))

print(v) # (0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25)

*The below is equivalent to the above.

def func():
    for x in range(6):
        yield x**2
v = func()

print(tuple(v))
# (0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25)

2D tuple: 

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]

v = tuple(tuple(y**2 for y in x) for x in sample)

print(v) # ((0, 1, 4), (9, 16, 25))

*The below is equivalent to the above.

def func():
    def func(x):
        for y in x:
            yield y**2

    for x in [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]:
        yield tuple(func(x))

v = tuple(func())

print(v)
# ((0, 1, 4), (9, 16, 25))

items.title: Comprehension in Python (1)
items.author: Super Kai (Kazuya Ito)
items.content:
Buy Me a Coffee☕

*Memos:

  • My post explains generator comprehension(expression) and tuple comprehension.

Comprehension is the concise way to create an iterable. There are list comprehension, set comprehension, dictionary(dict) comprehension and generator comprehension(expression). *There isn't tuple comprehension in a direct way in Python but there is in an indirect way.

<List Comprehension>

1D list: 

v = [x**2 for x in range(6)]

print(v) # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

*The below is equivalent to the above.

v = []

for x in range(6):
    v.append(x**2)

print(v) # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

2D list: 

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]

v = [[y**2 for y in x] for x in sample]

print(v) # [[0, 1, 4], [9, 16, 25]]

*The below is equivalent to the above.

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]
v = []

for i, x in enumerate(sample):
    v.append([])
    for y in x:
        v[i].append(y**2)

print(v) # [[0, 1, 4], [9, 16, 25]]

<Set Comprehension>

1D set: 

v = {x**2 for x in range(6)}

print(v) # {0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25}

*The below is equivalent to the above.

v = set()

for x in range(6):
    v.add(x**2)

print(v) # {0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25}

2D set: 

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]

v = {tuple(y**2 for y in x) for x in sample}

print(v) # {(9, 16, 25), (0, 1, 4)}

*The below is equivalent to the above.

sample = [[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]]
v = set()

for x in sample:
    def func(x):
        for y in x:
            yield y**2
    v.add(tuple(func(x)))

print(v) # {(9, 16, 25), (0, 1, 4)}

<Dictionary(Dict) Comprehension>

1D dictionary: 

v = {x:x**2 for x in range(6)}

print(v) # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16, 5: 25}

*The below is equivalent to the above.

v = {}

for x in range(6):
    v.update({x:x**2})

print(v) # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16, 5: 25}

2D dictionary:

*Memos:

  • A key can have a tuple, but not a list, set and dictionary. 
sample = ((0, 1, 2), (3, 4, 5))

v = {x: {y:y**2 for y in x} for x in sample}

print(v)
# {(0, 1, 2): {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4}, (3, 4, 5): {3: 9, 4: 16, 5: 25}}

*The below is equivalent to the above.

sample = ((0, 1, 2), (3, 4, 5))
v = {}

for x in sample:
    v.update({x:{}})
    for y in x:
        v[x].update({y:y**2})

print(v) # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16}
# {(0, 1, 2): {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4}, (3, 4, 5): {3: 9, 4: 16, 5: 25}}

items.title: Linear Algebra for Machine Learning: A Practical Guide
items.author: Dev Patel
items.content:
Imagine trying to navigate a complex city without a map. You might stumble upon your destination eventually, but it would be inefficient and prone to errors. Similarly, tackling machine learning problems without a solid understanding of linear algebra is like navigating without a map. Linear algebra provides the essential mathematical framework for many core ML algorithms, allowing for efficient and accurate solutions. This article explores the crucial role of linear algebra in machine learning, providing practical examples and a guide to get you started.

Core Concepts with Practical Examples

Linear algebra revolves around vectors and matrices. A vector is a list of numbers, often representing a point in space. A matrix is a grid of numbers, representing transformations or relationships between vectors. Let's explore some key concepts:

Vector Operations: Adding, subtracting, and multiplying vectors are fundamental. Scalar multiplication involves multiplying each element of a vector by a single number.

```python import numpy as np

Vector addition

v1 = np.array([1, 2, 3])
v2 = np.array([4, 5, 6])
v_sum = v1 + v2

Output: [5 7 9]

Scalar multiplication

scalar = 2
v_scaled = scalar * v1

Output: [2 4 6] 




Matrix Operations: Matrix multiplication is a more complex operation, involving the dot product of rows and columns. It's crucial for transformations in image processing and neural networks.

python # Matrix multiplication
matrix_A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]]) 
matrix_B = np.array([[5, 6], [7, 8]]) 
matrix_product = np.dot(matrix_A, matrix_B)
# Output: [[19 22] [43 50]]

Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors: These are fundamental to dimensionality reduction techniques like Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Eigenvectors represent the directions in which a linear transformation stretches or shrinks space, and eigenvalues represent the scaling factors. Finding them often involves solving characteristic equations. Libraries like NumPy provide functions for this.

python # Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors (using NumPy's linag module) eigenvalues, eigenvectors
np.linalg.eig(matrix_A)
print("Eigenvalues:", eigenvalues)
print("Eigenvectors:", eigenvectors)

Real-World Applications

Linear algebra underpins numerous ML algorithms:

Image Recognition: Images are represented as matrices, and transformations like rotations and scaling are performed using matrix multiplication. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) heavily rely on matrix operations for feature extraction. For example, Google's image recognition system uses linear algebra extensively to process and classify images.
Natural Language Processing (NLP): Word embeddings, which represent words as vectors, are used in many NLP tasks. Cosine similarity, a measure of the angle between vectors, is used to determine the semantic similarity between words. Sentiment analysis models often use matrix operations to process textual data.
Recommendation Systems: Collaborative filtering algorithms use matrix factorization to predict user preferences. Netflix's recommendation engine uses similar techniques based on matrix factorization and singular value decomposition (SVD).
Machine Learning Models: Linear regression, support vector machines (SVMs), and neural networks all rely heavily on matrix operations for training and prediction. The backpropagation algorithm in neural networks, for instance, involves extensive matrix calculations for updating weights.

Getting Started Guide

To begin your journey with linear algebra for ML, you'll need:

  1. Python: The most popular language for ML.
  2. NumPy: A powerful library for numerical computation, including linear algebra operations.
  3. SciPy: Provides advanced scientific computing capabilities, including linear algebra functions beyond those in NumPy.

Install NumPy and SciPy using pip: pip install numpy scipy

Challenges and Best Practices

Computational Cost: Matrix operations, especially for large matrices, can be computationally expensive. Consider optimized algorithms and libraries.
Numerical Stability: Floating-point arithmetic can lead to inaccuracies. Use appropriate data types and algorithms to mitigate this.
Data Preprocessing: Proper data scaling and normalization are crucial for many linear algebra-based algorithms.

Always test your code thoroughly and validate your results.

Learning Resources

Official Documentation:
NumPy Documentation
SciPy Documentation
Pandas Documentation (for data manipulation)

Tutorials:
Khan Academy Linear Algebra
3Blue1Brown Linear Algebra
Towards Data Science Linear Algebra for ML

Video Courses:
Linear Algebra for Machine Learning (Coursera)
MIT OpenCourseware Linear Algebra

Communities: Stack Overflow (search for tags like numpy, linear-algebra, machine-learning)

Conclusion

Linear algebra is the foundation upon which many powerful machine learning algorithms are built. Mastering its core concepts, coupled with practical implementation using libraries like NumPy and SciPy, is crucial for any aspiring machine learning engineer. Start with the basics, practice regularly, and gradually explore more advanced topics to unlock the full potential of this vital mathematical tool. Continue your learning by exploring the resources listed above and tackling practical projects.

items.title: My_First_Project
items.author: T-Roy
items.content:
import random

def play_game():
print("Welcome to Rock, Paper, Scissors!")
print("Type 'rock', 'paper', or 'scissors' to play. Type 'quit' to exit.")

choices = ["rock", "paper", "scissors"]

while True:
    user_choice = input("Your choice: ").lower()
    if user_choice == "quit":
        print("Thanks for playing! Goodbye!")
        break

    if user_choice not in choices:
        print("Invalid choice. Please try again.")
        continue

    computer_choice = random.choice(choices)
    print(f"Computer chose: {computer_choice}")

    if user_choice == computer_choice:
        print("It's a tie!")
    elif (user_choice == "rock" and computer_choice == "scissors") or \
         (user_choice == "paper" and computer_choice == "rock") or \
         (user_choice == "scissors" and computer_choice == "paper"):
        print("You win!")
    else:
        print("You lose!")

items.title: Stop Wasting Time Converting cURL Commands Manually - Here's How I Automated It
items.author: SiBo
items.content:
Converting cURL commands to programming languages is one of those tedious tasks every developer faces. Whether you're integrating a new API, debugging webhooks, or following documentation examples, we've all been there - staring at a cURL command and manually translating it to our preferred language.

After doing this for the hundredth time, I decided there had to be a better way. That's when I built 1, and it's been saving developers hours of time ever since.

The Problem We All Face 😤

Picture this scenario (I bet you've been here):

  1. You find API documentation with cURL examples
  2. You need it in Python/JavaScript/Go/whatever
  3. You spend 10-15 minutes manually converting headers, data, and parameters
  4. You debug syntax errors because you missed a quote or comma
  5. Repeat for every single endpoint

Here's a real example from a recent API integration:

curl -X POST https://api.stripe.com/v1/customers \
  -u sk_test_4eC39HqLyjWDarjtT1zdp7dc: \
  -d "email=customer@example.com" \
  -d "description=My test customer" \
  -d "payment_method=pm_card_visa" \
  -d "invoice_settings[default_payment_method]=pm_card_visa"

Now imagine converting this to Python manually. Headers, authentication, form data encoding... it's a pain!

The Solution That Changes Everything ⚡

Instead of manual conversion, here's what you can do:

  1. Copy your cURL command
  2. Paste it into 1
  3. Select your target language
  4. Copy the converted code

That's it. The same Stripe example becomes this in Python:

import requests

url = "https://api.stripe.com/v1/customers"

payload = {
    "email": "customer@example.com",
    "description": "My test customer",
    "payment_method": "pm_card_visa",
    "invoice_settings[default_payment_method]": "pm_card_visa"
}

headers = {
    "Authorization": "Basic c2tfdGVzdF80ZUMzOUhxTHlqV0Rhcmp0VDF6ZHA3ZGM6"
}

response = requests.post(url, data=payload, headers=headers)

print(response.text)

Real-World Use Cases 🛠️

1. API Documentation to Code

Most API docs provide cURL examples. With curl-to.com, you can instantly convert them to:

  • Python (requests)
  • JavaScript (fetch, axios)
  • PHP (cURL, Guzzle)
  • Go (net/http)
  • Ruby (net::http)
  • And 15+ more languages

2. Debugging Webhooks

Got a webhook that's not working? Convert the cURL test command to your language and debug with proper IDE support, breakpoints, and logging.

3. Team Collaboration

Share cURL commands with your team, and everyone can convert to their preferred language. No more "Can someone convert this to Node.js for me?" messages.

Advanced Features That Save Even More Time 🎯

Handle Complex Authentication 

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" \
     -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
     https://api.example.com/data

Automatically converts to proper headers in any language.

File Uploads Made Simple 

curl -X POST \
  -F "file=@/path/to/file.jpg" \
  -F "description=Profile photo" \
  https://api.example.com/upload

Generates correct multipart/form-data code for your language.

JSON Payload Formatting 

curl -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"John","age":30,"city":"NYC"}' \
  https://api.example.com/users

Properly formats JSON payloads with correct content types.

Pro Tips for Maximum Productivity 💡

1. Bookmark for Quick Access

Add curl-to.com to your bookmarks bar for instant access when browsing API docs.

2. Use with Browser DevTools

Copy requests from Chrome/Firefox DevTools as cURL, then convert to code:

  • Right-click on request → Copy → Copy as cURL
  • Paste into curl-to.com
  • Get clean code instantly

3. Build Test Suites Faster

Converting API examples to test code? Use curl-to.com to quickly generate test cases in your testing framework's language.

Why Developers Love It ❤️

  • No Sign-up Required: Just paste and convert
  • Privacy-Focused: Your commands aren't stored or logged
  • Always Updated: Supports latest syntax and best practices
  • Mobile-Friendly: Convert on the go from your phone
  • Free Forever: No premium tiers or limitations

Common Conversions Comparison

Here's a quick comparison of how the same request looks in different languages:

Original cURL:

curl -X GET "https://api.github.com/users/octocat" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json"

JavaScript (fetch):

fetch("https://api.github.com/users/octocat", {
  method: "GET",
  headers: {
    "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"
  }
})
.then(response => response.json())
.then(data => console.log(data));

Python (requests):

import requests

headers = {
    'Accept': 'application/vnd.github.v3+json',
}

response = requests.get('https://api.github.com/users/octocat', headers=headers)
print(response.json())

Start Saving Time Today 🎉

Next time you see a cURL command in documentation, don't waste time manually converting it. Head to 1 and get clean, working code in seconds.

What's your most painful API integration experience? Drop a comment below - I'd love to hear your stories and maybe add features to make your life easier!

If you found this helpful, give it a ❤️ and share with your team. Follow me for more developer productivity tips!

Building tools that make developers' lives easier. Creator of curl-to.com and passionate about developer experience.

items.title: Porque Flask é o framework mais didático que já existiu
items.author: Vitor Rezende Moura
items.content:
Primeiro, vamos com calma

Antes de tudo, vamos com calma. Sei que esse tipo de afirmação pode gerar problemas de entendimento e até certo favoritismo em relação a tecnologias. Entretanto, precisamos ter em mente as necessidades que nossas ferramentas suprem e por que devemos usá-las.

Nos primórdios, veio o MVC...

Nos primórdios do desenvolvimento web, quando queríamos conectar e criar ferramentas, não existiam conceitos como micro-frontends, SaaS, microservices ou nada parecido. O mais próximo disso eram aplicações monolíticas, desenvolvidas com stacks que seguiam arquiteturas “quadradas” e bem estabelecidas. Um exemplo clássico é a infame stack LAMP:

  • Linux
  • Apache
  • MySQL
  • PHP

Nessa época, era comum seguir a arquitetura MVC (Model-View-Controller), onde:

  • A View apresentava os dados e recebia as entradas dos usuários;
  • O Model era responsável por manipular e armazenar os dados no banco de dados;
  • O Controller fazia o meio de campo entre a visualização e a lógica de negócio.

Com o avanço das aplicações e das necessidades da web moderna, surgiram outros modelos e paradigmas que possibilitaram o desenvolvimento de ferramentas mais complexas. Eles mudaram a forma como processamos dados, renderizamos interfaces e integramos diferentes sistemas.

Mas aqui vai um ponto importante:

Só porque o MVC é uma arquitetura antiga, não significa que está defasada.

Muito pelo contrário — a maioria dos sistemas microempresariais ou de escopo pessoal não precisa de soluções super complexas, que envolvam escalabilidade em tempo real, uso de Docker, pipelines no Jenkins ou alertas de sobrecarga enviados via Resend.

Nesses casos, um bom "feijão com arroz" feito com frameworks como Django, Flask, Ruby on Rails ou até mesmo Laravel já atende muito bem às necessidades. E melhor ainda: com mais simplicidade, produtividade e menos dor de cabeça.

Mas no final, por que Flask?

Apesar da afirmação que inicia este post, e do que foi apresentado até agora, pode parecer óbvio que vou "supostamente" apresentar argumentos embasados e bem direcionados sobre o porquê de um framework ser mais qualificado que outro para determinadas ações e necessidades. No entanto, proponho justamente o contrário.

Começando pelo Django e indo para o Flask, depois passando pelo Spring, desviando para o Express, voltando ao Spring e mantendo um flerte com o Flask, observei como — diferente das outras ferramentas que citei anteriormente — ele apresenta, propositalmente, uma curva de aprendizado muito menor para quem está começando. E essa curva cresce conforme as necessidades do sistema vão aparecendo, sem exigir a adoção imediata de padrões organizacionais rígidos, como no Django; padrões fixos de construção, como no Spring; ou até o uso constante de arrow functions e a quantidade avassaladora de decisões possíveis no Express.

No Flask, você pode construir desde aplicações completas com apenas um único arquivo de código, até sistemas grandes e organizados, que crescem de forma robusta e segura em várias camadas, utilizando padrões de projeto conhecidos e elementos independentes que se integram conforme necessário.

E o castelo de Lego vai se montando...

Temos dentro do Flask, além do conceito arquitetural do MVC, o conceito da possibilidade de criar elementos e usá-los por meio de extensões. Isso é um pensamento muito forte dentro da comunidade do framework e dentro dele mesmo, em que podemos resumir a:

Se não necessito dentro do meu projeto, não necessito instalar ele

Com isso, o framework vem com o mínimo necessário para ser construída uma aplicação Web, e conforme as necessidades vão surgindo, vamos adicionando extensões responsáveis por suprir essas necessidades, e integrar diretamente com nossa aplicação por meio da sua extensibilidade.

Um exemplo disso é que as extensões mais famosas são justamente extensões responsáveis por solucionar problemas simples, mas fundamentais na maioria dos projetos, como, por exemplo:

  • Flask-SQLAlchemy: Modelagem de dados e de entidades dentro do código
  • Flask-Login: Autenticação e Autorização
  • Flask-WTForms: Segurança, construção e responsabilidade com formulários
  • Flask-Bootstrap: Bootstrap de forma nativa dentro do Flask
  • Flask-SocketIO: Uso de WebSockets fácil e direto
  • Flask-Mail: Envio de e-mails do lado do servidor de forma automática e organizada

Com isso, pode vir um questionamento muito importante de ser falado:

Tá, mas os outros frameworks também possuem essas ferramentas e você não é obrigado a implantá-las logo de cara. Por que o Flask seria mais recomendado?

O fato do framework nem sequer possuir essas ferramentas, e não possuir uma estrutura necessária, faz com que cada elemento possa ser aprendido separadamente e de forma independente, sem que precisem ser feitas ressalvas, e sem que seja, a todo momento, instigada a necessidade de usar aquela determinada ferramenta no projeto com a ideia de "Ah... já tem, então vou aproveitar e implementar", e aquilo que era pra ser um CRUD simples virou um e-commerce com marketplace de camiseta do Vasco, até bitcoin do Paraguai.

E caso haja mais dúvidas da sua simplicidade de construção, é possível construir uma rota que retorna uma mensagem, com apenas 7 linhas (tendo em vista algumas necessidades que foram incluídas apenas para manter organização e qualidade).

from flask import Flask 

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
def index():
    return "Hello world!"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

Quem sabe no futuro...

Inclusive, penso que até mesmo aqui dentro do Tabnews, é possível aprender o Flask, somente por meio dos posts e dos textos -- e caso seja a vontade de vocês, irei fazer isso. Portanto, se você anseia em aprender Flask, ou ver essa saga tendo algum rumo, se expresse abaixo, e inclusive, peço que se expresse para concordar, e PRINCIPALMENTE discordar de mim...

items.title: 🔥 FastAPI in Production: Build, Scale & Deploy – Series A: Codebase Design
items.author: Mr Chike
items.content:
Welcome to Part 1 of the 3-part FastAPI Series

We're going to build a solid, scalable FastAPI codebase from the ground up. Along the way, we'll cover project structure, environment setup, dependency injection, and async I/O-bound API calls.

Whether you're working on monoliths, microservices, or event-driven systems, this guide is about laying a clean, modular, and solid foundation, one that’s been tested in real-world environments, not just spun up for a quick demo.

Table of Contents

  • 🧠 A Bit of Background
  • 🎯 What to Expect
  • 🗂️ Project Structure Overview
  • 🏗️ Project Initialization
  • 🔄 Request Lifecycle
  • 🧪 Testing the Gears
  • 🏁 Conclusion

🧠 A Bit of Background

Like many developers, I started with Python. I was eager to learn (still am), and dove into every tutorial I could find. It didn’t take long before I hit Tut🔥rial Hell, you know that endless loop where every new video feels like I've finally found the ch🙌sen one. Spoiler alert: it never was...

But that hell did prepare me well enough to land an internship. And to my surprise, I realized I was actually light years ahead of my peers.

That internship was a deep dive into the real world: a full-scale, SaaS, enterprise-grade KPI management system built with Django, serving over 100K users (with more being onboarded regularly). It was the first time I saw Python used at scale in a production environment and it completely changed how I thought about architecture and design.

Since then, I’ve worked on Python applications across different industries, helping teams scale their systems (and occasionally refactoring parts of legacy code).

Eventually, I started hearing about the latest kid on the block: FastAPI, the "speedster" of the Python ecosystem. I checked it out, watched a bunch of tutorials... and quickly noticed a pattern: a lot of them were just theory. Surface-level. Book-knowledge.

This series is my attempt to share what I’ve learned over the years about Python’s real capabilities in production.

🎯 What to Expect

This series is practical. It’s grounded in real-world experience and designed with ❤️ by yours truly, the host of this series, Mr. Chike👨🏾‍💻 to show you how to build systems that are modular, maintainable, and scalable from day one.

So enough talk.

Let’s get to work. 💥
📦 View Full Code on GitHub

🗂️ Project Structure Overview 

media_app/

base/                                  # Feature module
├── __init__.py                        # Python package initialization
├── router.py                          # Defines HTTP API endpoints and maps them to controller functions
├── controller.py                      # Handles request-response cycle; delegates business logic to services
├── service.py                         # Core business logic for async I/O operations
├── model.py                           # SQLAlchemy ORM models representing database tables
├── schema.py                          # Pydantic models for input validation and output serialization
├── dependencies.py                    # Module-specific DI components like authentication and DB sessions
├── tasks.py                           # Core business logic for CPU-bound operations

├── movies/                            # Movie feature module

├── tv_series/                         # TV series feature module

├── static/                            # (Optional) Static files (e.g., images, CSS)

├── templates/                         # (Optional) Jinja2 or HTML templates for frontend rendering

├── docs/                              # (Optional) API documentation, design specs, or OpenAPI enhancements

├── shared/                            # Project-wide shared codebase
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── config/                        # Environment configuration setup
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   └── settings.py                # Pydantic-based config management
│   ├── dependencies/                  # Shared DI functions (e.g., auth, DB session)
│   ├── middleware/                    # Global middlewares (e.g., logging, error handling)
│   ├── services/                      # Reusable services
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── external_apis/             # Third-party integrations (e.g., TMDB, IMDB)
│   │   └── internal_operations/       # CPU-intensive logic, background tasks
│   └── utils/                         # Generic helpers (e.g., slugify, formatters)

├── scripts/                           # Developer or DevOps utilities
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── sanity_check.py                # A friendly reminder not to lose your mind while debugging

├── tests/                             # # Unit, Integration, System, and End-to-End (E2E) tests for app modules
│   ├── __init__.py

├── .example.env                       # Template for environment variables (e.g., DB_URL, API_KEY)
├── .coveragerc                        # Code coverage settings
├── .gitignore                         # Files and folders ignored by Git
├── main.py                            # FastAPI application entrypoint
├── pytest.ini                         # Pytest configuration
├── requirements.txt                   # Python dependency list
├── JOURNAL.md                         # Development log: issues faced, solutions, and resources
└── README.md                          # Project overview, setup, and usage

🏗️ Project Initialization

We’ll start by setting up the project folder and running an initialization script to scaffold the directories and files we’ll use throughout the article.

You can find the full setup.sh script in the GitHub repository.

# Create the main project folder and initialize Git
mkdir media_app && cd media_app && git init

# Add and make the setup script executable
touch setup.sh && chmod +x setup.sh

# Update 'setup.sh' with your GitHub repository link, then run the setup script to scaffold the project
./setup.sh

# Activate your Python virtual environment (if applicable)
source env/bin/activate

Now update main.py with the content from this file, then start the project by running:

uvicorn main:app --reload --port 8000

This will launch the app with live reload enabled on port 8000. (Feel free to change the port to whatever your majesty desires.)

I know firsthand how frustrating it can be to read a technical article. it's almost like the developer assumes you're inside their head and completely understand all the complexities.

That's exactly why I'm taking my time to explain things clearly and step by step.

Now, let’s move on to the next phase...

🔄 Request Lifecycle

  • The router receives an HTTP request from the user and forwards it to a controller.

  • The controller validates the request data often using schemas for input validation and output serialization, then calls the appropriate service or tasks function.

  • The service handles the core business logic for async I/O operations, such as CRUD operations on the database (using model.py) or interactions with external APIs.

  • When needed, the service also uses components from dependencies.py, such as authentication or database session injection, to get the job done.

  • For CPU-bound or long-running operations, the service delegates the work to tasks.py. These tasks are executed asynchronously using Celery, with a broker like Redis or RabbitMQ, to keep the user experience smooth. (Covered in Part 2 of this series)

😒 No one loves waiting, so the heavy lifting is done in the background while the user gets a quick response and moves on with their day.

  • Once processing is complete, the service returns the result to the controller, which formats the response using schemas, and sends it back through the router to the client.

Now that we understand the overall process, let’s break down what it actually looks like in motion.

When a user hits the movie endpoint, here’s what's going on in the background:

App Entry (main.py):
The FastAPI app initializes and mounts the movie_router under /movies on Line 13. This means all movie-related routes start with /movies.

Router Layer (movies/router.py):
The router defines specific HTTP routes and forwards incoming requests to the controller. For example, a POST request to /movies/omdb/ hits the router’s get_movie method on Line 11.

Controller Layer (movies/controller.py):
The controller acts as a middleman by receiving the validated request data, calls the service layer to handle business logic, and formats the response to send back to the client as seen from Line 10.

Service Layer (movies/service.py):
This layer constructs the external API URL and delegates the task of fetching data to the external API service. It handles errors gracefully, logging issues for developers and returning user-friendly messages if something goes wrong as seen from Line 19.

External API Call (shared/services/external_apis/omdb_movies.py):
The actual HTTP request to the OMDB API is made here using httpx. This function focuses on making reliable API calls and raising exceptions on errors, which the service layer catches as seen from Line 6.

Once the response is received from the external service, it’s returned back through the chain:

→ from fetch_movie_omdb to
→ get_movie_details (service) to
→ the controller, and finally
→ back to the client via the router.

This layered, modular design ensures each part has a clear responsibility, making the codebase easier to maintain, test, and scale.

Now speaking of testing… I just hope the length of this article isn’t unit-testing your patience. 🧪🤯

🧪 Testing the Gears

In this section, we’ll be blending three powerful tools:

  • pytest to configure and run tests across the entire module,

  • unittest to drill down into individual units, and

  • coverage to generate reports on what’s been tested and what hasn’t.

This hybrid approach ensures both broad and deep testing of the system while keeping things modular, clear, and production-ready.

We'll start by configuring pytest.ini alongside coverage so it can automatically detect test files and report on which parts of the codebase are covered.

Next, we’ll configure .coveragerc to omit files that don’t need to be tested like (e.g, __init.py, model.py) and other boilerplate or non-critical modules, so the reports remain clean, focused and provide meaningful insights.

Now that we’ve done that, we can test if the configuration is properly set up by running the command: pytest

(env) mrchike@practice:~/code/contributions/education/media_app$ pytest
==================================================== test session starts ====================================================
platform linux -- Python 3.10.12, pytest-8.3.5, pluggy-1.6.0
rootdir: /home/mrchike/code/contributions/education/media_app
configfile: pytest.ini
testpaths: tests/
plugins: anyio-4.9.0, cov-6.1.1
collected 0 items                                                                                                           
/home/mrchike/code/contributions/education/media_app/env/lib/python3.10/site-packages/coverage/control.py:915: CoverageWarning: No data was collected. (no-data-collected)
  self._warn("No data was collected.", slug="no-data-collected")

====================================================== tests coverage =======================================================
_____________________________________ coverage: platform linux, python 3.10.12-final-0 ______________________________________

Name                   Stmts   Miss  Cover   Missing
----------------------------------------------------
movies/controller.py      10     10     0%   1-13
movies/service.py         19     19     0%   1-32
movies/tasks.py            0      0   100%
----------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                     29     29     0%
=================================================== no tests ran in 0.16s ===================================================

And just like that we have this beautiful display of Missing tests you've not attended to leading us to the next step ...

Unit testing

Now that we know which files and lines of code need testing from the coverage report, we will proceed with writing tests for controller.py and service.py respectively

(env) mrchike@practice:~/code/contributions/education/media_app$ pytest
================================ test session starts =================================
platform linux -- Python 3.10.12, pytest-8.3.5, pluggy-1.6.0
rootdir: /home/mrchike/code/contributions/education/media_app
configfile: pytest.ini
testpaths: tests/
plugins: anyio-4.9.0, cov-6.1.1
collected 2 items                                                                    

tests/movies/test_controller.py ..                                             [100%]

=================================== tests coverage ===================================
__________________ coverage: platform linux, python 3.10.12-final-0 __________________

Name                   Stmts   Miss  Cover   Missing
----------------------------------------------------
movies/controller.py      10      0   100%
movies/service.py         19     10    47%   20-32
movies/tasks.py            0      0   100%
----------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                     29     10    66%
================================= 2 passed in 2.35s ==================================


(env) mrchike@practice:~/code/contributions/education/media_app$ pytest
========================================================= test session starts ==========================================================
platform linux -- Python 3.10.12, pytest-8.3.5, pluggy-1.6.0
rootdir: /home/mrchike/code/contributions/education/media_app
configfile: pytest.ini
testpaths: tests/
plugins: anyio-4.9.0, cov-6.1.1
collected 4 items                                                                                                                      

tests/movies/test_controller.py ..                                                                                               [ 50%]
tests/movies/test_service.py ..                                                                                                  [100%]

============================================================ tests coverage ============================================================
___________________________________________ coverage: platform linux, python 3.10.12-final-0 ___________________________________________

Name                   Stmts   Miss  Cover   Missing
----------------------------------------------------
movies/controller.py      10      0   100%
movies/service.py         19      0   100%   
movies/tasks.py            0      0   100%
----------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                     29      0   100%
========================================================== 4 passed in 2.42s ===========================================================

🏁 Conclusion

In this first part of the series, we've laid the groundwork for building a scalable, production-ready FastAPI application. We covered essential concepts like project structure, modularity, and the flow of requests through various layers of the app. Most importantly, we set up testing strategies to ensure our code is robust and maintainable.

Quick Recap:

  • Project Structure: We’ve created a clean, scalable folder structure that’s ready for real-world growth.
  • Request Flow: We broke down how requests travel through the router, controller, and service layers, ensuring clear responsibility separation.
  • Testing: We integrated pytest, unittest, and coverage, and wrote tests to make sure the code performs as expected.

With this solid foundation, we’re well-equipped to scale up the app and handle more advanced features in Part 2, such as background tasks for CPU-bound operations. So, stay tuned for that!

💡 Enjoyed this article? Connect with me on:

  • 💼 LinkedIn
  • 🐙 GitHub.

Your support means a lot, if you’d like to buy me a coffee ☕️ to keep me fueled, feel free to check out this link. Your generosity would go a long way in helping me continue to create content like this.

Until next time, happy coding! 👨🏾‍💻🚀

Previously Written Articles:
